text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY — OUTPATIENT CLINIC NOTE
Adjoining Department: Gastrointestinal Oncology
Visit Type: Follow-up — Adjuvant Chemotherapy Completion / Transition to Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 67-year-old retired pipe fitter presenting for a scheduled oncology follow-up visit. He has completed all 6 planned cycles of adjuvant gem/Xeloda (APACT regimen) following his Whipple procedure, and today's visit is dedicated to end-of-treatment assessment, review of recent laboratory data, and formal transition to the surveillance phase of his care. He reports feeling "better than I expected," though he continues to experience residual fatigue and neuropathy in his feet.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski has an extensively treated history of borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0, Stage III at presentation, ultimately pathologically staged ypT2N1M0 post-treatment), initially diagnosed approximately 16-17 months ago when he presented with painless obstructive jaundice, a weight loss of approximately 8 kg, and a markedly elevated CA 19-9 of 1200 U/mL. His oncologic treatment course has been lengthy and eventful:

(1) He completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (with Eloxatin reduced to 68 mg/m2 at cycle 5 for cumulative neuropathy, and Camptosar reduced to 135 mg/m2 after cycle 7 hospitalization for grade 3 diarrhea). His CA 19-9 decreased from 1200 to 85 U/mL by end of FOLFIRINOX; the primary tumor decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with improvement in vascular abutment of the superior mesenteric artery.

(2) He then received consolidative chemoradiation — 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions of IMRT with concurrent cape (reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID due to hand-foot syndrome). CA 19-9 declined further to 62 U/mL post-radiation; tumor size decreased to 2.1 cm on pre-surgical restaging CT.

(3) He underwent open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental SMV resection and primary end-to-end reconstruction. R0 resection was achieved on all margins. Final pathology: 2.0 cm residual moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma, CAP treatment response grade 2, 3 of 18 lymph nodes positive (ypT2N1M0, Stage IIB). Perineural invasion present. Lymphovascular invasion present. His postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula (ISGPS criteria) requiring readmission, TPN for approximately 3 weeks, IV antibiotics, octreotide, and CT-guided drain repositioning. Full resolution of the fistula was achieved by approximately postoperative week 10, necessitating a delay in starting adjuvant therapy.

(4) He initiated adjuvant Gemzar/cape (APACT) at 10 weeks postoperatively, starting at full doses (gem 1000 mg/m2 days 1, 8, 15; cape 830 mg/m2 BID days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle). Cycle 2 required cape dose reduction to 750 mg/m2 BID for grade 1-2 hand-foot syndrome, and Gemzar day 15 was held in cycle 2 due to thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 88 x 10^9/L). Transaminase elevation was transient and grade 1. He otherwise managed through cycles 1 through 6 without additional major dose modifications, with manageable grade 2 fatigue and grade 1-2 nausea.

He completed cycle 6 of adjuvant Gemzar/Xeloda approximately 3 weeks ago. He reports that since completing chemotherapy, his energy has modestly improved, though he remains fatigued compared to his pre-diagnosis baseline. He has grade 1-2 numbness and tingling in bilateral feet (greater than hands), which he notes has not worsened since Eloxatin-containing therapy was discontinued over a year ago. He reports adequate glycemic control on his insulin regimen with no hypoglycemic episodes this past month. He is tolerating oral meals with the aid of pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon). He denies new abdominal pain, jaundice, back pain, new weight loss, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, fevers, or night sweats. He has not noted any new masses or swollen lymph nodes. Bowel habits are somewhat loose but stable, which he attributes to his altered anatomy post-Whipple.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Residual fatigue (moderate); no fevers, night sweats, or new significant weight changes over the past month. Weight is improved from post-fistula nadir, trending toward pre-diagnosis baseline.
- Gastrointestinal: Loose stools 2-3x daily, stable, consistent with post-Whipple anatomy and exocrine insufficiency managed with enzyme replacement. No nausea at present (now off chemotherapy). No vomiting, no abdominal pain, no early satiety beyond mild. No jaundice, no dark urine, no acholic stools.
- Neurological: Bilateral foot numbness and tingling, grade 1-2, stable. Mild impairment of fine motor tasks with hands, improved compared to peak during FOLFIRINOX. On duloxetine 30 mg daily for neuropathic symptom management (started during adjuvant phase).
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus managed with basal-bolus insulin (glargine + aspart). Home glucose logs reviewed — fasting glucose readings generally 110-140 mg/dL; some post-meal excursions to 180 mg/dL. Being followed by endocrinology in parallel.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized deconditioning, improved with physical activity.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no dyspnea. Hypertension managed per outpatient internal medicine.
- Pulmonary: No cough, no dyspnea on exertion beyond mild deconditioning.
- Skin: Hand-foot syndrome resolved. No new skin lesions.
- Hematologic: No easy bruising or bleeding noted.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria.
- Psychiatric: Patient describes appropriate mood, optimistic about completion of treatment, and engaged in discussions about surveillance.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (summarized; full history documented in prior consult notes)
1. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma — borderline resectable, KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A — as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus (now post-pancreatectomy insulin-dependent diabetes)
3. Hypertension
4. Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (post-Whipple)
5. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (Eloxatin-related, predominantly sensory)
6. Prior Grade B pancreatic fistula (resolved)
7. Prior biliary stent placement (ERCP, plastic stent, at time of diagnosis; no longer in situ)

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- Open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) with segmental SMV resection and end-to-end reconstruction
- Biliary stent placement (ERCP)

FAMILY HISTORY
- No family history of pancreatic cancer
- Father: hypertension, coronary artery disease
- Mother: type 2 diabetes
- No known hereditary cancer syndromes

SOCIAL HISTORY
- Retired pipe fitter
- Former smoker: 30 pack-year history; quit 15 years prior to diagnosis
- No current alcohol use
- Married, lives at home with spouse; two adult children provide support
- No illicit drug use
- Attends outpatient physical therapy for deconditioning and neuropathy

GERMLINE TESTING
- Germline BRCA1, BRCA2, and ATM testing: results previously noted as pending at the time of the initial MDT board; per chart review, germline testing returned NEGATIVE for pathogenic variants in BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, and other NCCN-recommended genes (confirmed no hereditary predisposition identified).

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS (current)
1. Insulin glargine (Lantus) — basal insulin, dosed per endocrinology
2. Insulin aspart (NovoLog) — mealtime insulin, per sliding scale
3. Pancreatic enzyme replacement: Creon 36,000 units — 3 capsules with meals, 1-2 with snacks
4. Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 30 mg orally once daily — for chemotherapy-induced neuropathy
5. Lisinopril — for hypertension (dose managed by PCP/internal medicine)
6. Ondansetron 4 mg — PRN nausea (prescribed during adjuvant; minimal current use)
7. Loperamide — PRN loose stools
8. Multivitamin
9. Vitamin D 1000 IU daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Mr. Kowalski is a well-appearing, moderately-built male in no acute distress, alert and oriented x3, conversational, slightly tired-appearing but interactive and pleasant. He appears mildly cachectic compared to his pre-illness baseline but has clearly improved from his prior visits during active chemotherapy.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg; Heart rate 72 beats/min; Respiratory rate 16/min; Temperature 98.1°F (36.7°C); SpO2 97% on room air; Weight 74.2 kg (prior visit: 73.1 kg — modest improvement). BMI approximately 23.4 kg/m2.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No oral lesions. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.

Abdomen: Well-healed midline laparotomy scar. No jaundice. Abdomen soft, mildly scaphoid. No palpable mass in the epigastrium or right upper quadrant. No hepatomegaly. Mild diffuse tenderness to deep palpation in the mid-epigastric region, which the patient reports is his chronic post-surgical baseline. No guarding, no rebound. Normal bowel sounds. No ascites by percussion or palpation.

Extremities: Warm and well-perfused. Trace bilateral ankle edema (1+), unchanged. Bilateral lower extremity neurological exam: Diminished pinprick sensation in both feet to mid-ankle level, consistent with stocking distribution sensory neuropathy. Vibration sense mildly reduced at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact. Reflexes diminished at bilateral Achilles tendons, present at patellar tendons. No motor deficits in lower extremities. Upper extremity: Mild reduction in pinprick sensation in distal fingers bilaterally. Grip strength 4/5 bilaterally.

Skin: No palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome resolved). No radiation dermatitis residua. No new suspicious skin lesions.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x 10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild anemia, stable from prior — consistent with chronic disease/post-treatment effect)
- Platelets: 196 x 10^9/L (normal, recovered from nadir during adjuvant therapy)
- ANC: 3.2 x 10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
- ALT: 28 U/L (normal; previously transiently elevated during adjuvant — now fully normalized)
- AST: 24 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 78 U/L (normal)
- Albumin: 3.5 g/dL (low-normal; slightly improved from prior)
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 15 U/mL (normal range ≤37 U/mL; down from 1200 at diagnosis; has been trending in normal range since post-Whipple period)
- CEA: 2.2 ng/dL (normal; was 4.8 at diagnosis)

Glycemic:
- HbA1c: 7.8% (improved glycemic control compared to prior; endocrinology following; per patient, consistent with home glucose log trends)
- Fasting glucose today: 124 mg/dL

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (obtained at approximately 3 months post-Whipple, during mid-adjuvant phase): Showed expected post-Whipple anatomy, no recurrence in the surgical bed, no hepatic lesions, no peritoneal implants, no lymphadenopathy, no ascites. CA 19-9 at that time was 18 U/mL. No evidence of disease. A new surveillance CT has been ordered today and will be performed within the next 2-3 weeks as the first post-adjuvant imaging study.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 67-year-old man with a history of KRAS G12V-mutated, TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A-co-mutated, MSS, HRD-negative borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma who has now completed all planned multimodality treatment, including neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles, consolidative chemoradiation with cape, R0 Whipple procedure with SMV reconstruction (ypT2N1M0, Stage IIB, CAP response grade 2), and adjuvant gem/cape x 6 cycles (APACT regimen). His CA 19-9 is now 15 U/mL (well within normal range), down from a peak of 1200 U/mL at diagnosis. He is currently without evidence of disease (NED) and is transitioning to formal surveillance.

This is a significant milestone, and I spent considerable time discussing this with Mr. Kowalski and his spouse who accompanied him today. The trajectory from borderline resectable disease with markedly elevated CA 19-9 to a successful R0 resection and now NED status at the end of adjuvant therapy represents an excellent overall response to a rigorous treatment program. At the same time, I was transparent with him regarding the high relapse risk associated with his molecular profile — specifically, SMAD4 loss is a marker associated with propensity for systemic metastatic relapse, and 3 positive lymph nodes despite neoadjuvant therapy speaks to the biological aggressiveness of the disease. The importance of consistent, vigilant surveillance cannot be overstated.

1. ONCOLOGIC STATUS — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE
   - CA 19-9 15 U/mL, trending in the normal range since the post-surgical period.
   - Last imaging (approximately 3 months post-Whipple) was clean. A new surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast is ordered for approximately 3 weeks from now (the first post-adjuvant surveillance scan). This will be reviewed carefully for any locoregional recurrence in the pancreatic bed/hepaticojejunostomy region, hepatic metastases, peritoneal implants, or new lymphadenopathy.
   - Surveillance schedule moving forward per NCCN guidelines: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3-4 months for the first 2 years following surgery, then every 6 months for years 3-5. CA 19-9 to be checked with each surveillance visit (every 3 months).
   - Patient counseled that any new symptoms — particularly new-onset back pain, abdominal pain, jaundice, unintentional weight loss, or new anorexia — should prompt urgent contact with our office rather than waiting for the next scheduled appointment.

2. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (CIPN)
   - Grade 1-2 sensory neuropathy in bilateral feet, residual from Eloxatin (completed more than 12 months ago). Neuropathy in hands has improved to grade 1. Given that oxali was the causative agent and has been discontinued for well over a year, I expect gradual continued improvement, though some permanent deficits are possible.
   - Duloxetine 30 mg daily is being continued; I discussed the option of uptitrating to 60 mg daily given evidence for moderate benefit in CIPN (ASCO guidelines). Patient will try 60 mg daily starting next month and report back. Referral for ongoing physical therapy/occupational therapy for balance training and neuropathy management maintained.
   - I reviewed the neurology follow-up note in the chart; neurology agrees with current management and recommends B12 check — obtained today; will be available prior to next visit.

3. POST-PANCREATECTOMY DIABETES / GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT
   - HbA1c 7.8% today, improved from prior. Patient is being actively co-managed by endocrinology. Basal-bolus insulin regimen is appropriate for post-pancreatectomy (type 3c) diabetes. Metformin remains held given the pancreatectomy and altered GI physiology.
   - I reinforced to the patient the importance of not developing hypoglycemia given the absent glucagon response in total/near-total pancreatectomy physiology — he should alert our team or endocrinology if he experiences any hypoglycemic episodes.
   - Endocrinology follow-up is next month.

4. EXOCRINE PANCREATIC INSUFFICIENCY
   - Patient is maintained on Creon 36,000 units (3 capsules with meals, 1-2 capsules with snacks). He reports adequate management of steatorrhea; stools remain somewhat loose but tolerable. Weight is slowly recovering. Nutritional counseling was offered and a follow-up dietitian referral placed.
   - Albumin 3.5 g/dL — low-normal but improving. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (vitamins A, D, E, K) was reviewed; patient currently takes vitamin D 1000 IU. I recommended adding a comprehensive fat-soluble multivitamin supplement given the ongoing exocrine deficiency and will recheck nutritional labs (prealbumin, fat-soluble vitamins, B12) at next visit.

5. HYPERTENSION
   - Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg today, well-controlled on lisinopril. Continuing outpatient primary care management.

6. CARDIOVASCULAR / PREVENTIVE HEALTH
   - Patient is a former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 15 years ago). Low-dose CT lung cancer screening is appropriate; he was previously enrolled in a screening program and this will be coordinated through his PCP now that active cancer treatment has concluded.
   - Encouraged continued physical activity, Mediterranean-style diet adapted for post-Whipple anatomy, and smoking cessation reinforcement (already achieved).

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL / QUALITY OF LIFE
   - Mr. Kowalski expressed mixed emotions today — relief at completing treatment, but anxiety about what "not being on treatment" means in terms of disease control. I spent approximately 15 minutes discussing the rationale for the surveillance-only approach, the current NED status, and the importance of quality of life during this phase. Referral to the survivorship clinic was placed; the survivorship team will address cancer-related fatigue, CIPN, nutritional rehabilitation, and psychological support.
   - His wife was present and engaged; both were given written information about the NCCN pancreatic cancer survivorship guidelines and our clinic's surveillance protocol.

FOLLOW-UP: Return to clinic in 3 months with results of upcoming surveillance CT and repeat CA 19-9. Sooner if new symptoms arise.

Time spent: 45 minutes, greater than 50% in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by: Attending Physician, Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
